era_context: The Carolingian Empire is in advanced fragmentation. Charles the Bald rules the West Frankish Kingdom, Louis the German rules the East Frankish Kingdom, and Lothar II rules the Middle Kingdom (Lotharingia). The Byzantine Empire under Basil I is in the midst of cultural and military revival. The Abbasid Caliphate is politically fractured but intellectually vibrant. In China, the Tang Dynasty is in its final decades. Japan's Heian period continues with the Fujiwara family's influence growing. The Viking Age is in full force.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas: no written records; archaeological dating imprecise

events.title: Death of Lothar II; succession dispute over Lotharingia
events.description: Lothar II, King of the Middle Kingdom (Lotharingia), dies in 871 CE. His death triggers a succession dispute, as both Charles the Bald of the West Frankish Kingdom and Louis the German of the East Frankish Kingdom claim the territory. The dispute over Lotharingia will persist for decades and will be a major source of conflict among the Frankish kingdoms. The death of Lothar II marks a crucial moment in the fragmentation of the Carolingian Empire.
key_figures: Charles the Bald (West Frankish King, r. 843–877 CE)
Louis the German (East Frankish King, r. 843–873 CE)
events.certainty_note: Lothar II's death in 871 CE is well-documented in contemporary Frankish annals. The date is certain.
cross_references: Leads to the Treaty of Meerssen (872 CE); contributes to ongoing Frankish territorial disputes

events.title: Byzantine naval operations in the Aegean against Arab pirates
events.description: The Byzantine Empire, under Emperor Basil I, conducts naval operations against Arab pirates and raiders in the Aegean Sea. These operations are part of the broader Byzantine military revival of the Macedonian Renaissance. The Byzantines are attempting to secure their maritime trade routes and reduce Arab piracy in the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine naval operations in the Aegean during the 870s are attested, but specific operations for 871 CE are less precisely documented. The year is assigned based on the pattern of Byzantine military activity in this decade.
cross_references: Part of broader Byzantine military revival under Basil I

events.title: Continued Tang Dynasty decline; regional military governors consolidate power
events.description: The Tang Dynasty continues its decline in 871 CE. Regional military governors (jiedushi) are consolidating power independently of the central court. The imperial government's ability to collect taxes and maintain order is severely compromised. The Huang Chao Rebellion, which will erupt in the mid-870s, is being preceded by widespread banditry and local unrest. The Tang Dynasty's collapse is now inevitable, though it will not be formally declared until 907 CE.
key_figures: Emperor Xizong of Tang (r. 873–888 CE; not yet emperor in 871, but his father Emperor Yizong is ruling)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang
Tang official records and court chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Tang Dynasty's decline and the consolidation of power by regional military governors is well-documented in Chinese historical sources. The process is gradual, but by 871 CE, the trend is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader Tang Dynasty decline; leads to the dynasty's collapse (907 CE)

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Mu'tamid assumes power; attempts to restore central authority
events.description: Al-Mu'tamid becomes Abbasid Caliph in 870 CE (or 256 AH in the Islamic calendar), succeeding his brother al-Musta'in. Al-Mu'tamid attempts to restore central authority to the Caliphate, but his efforts are hampered by the autonomy of regional emirs and the power of the Turkish slave-soldiers (ghulams) who dominate the court. Despite his efforts, the Caliphate's political fragmentation continues. Al-Mu'tamid's reign (870–892 CE) is marked by internal conflict and the gradual loss of central authority.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: Al-Mu'tamid's assumption of power is well-documented in Islamic historical sources. The date is certain (870 CE in the Gregorian calendar).
cross_references: Part of broader Abbasid Caliphate decline; relates to the eventual rise of autonomous dynasties

disconfirming_evidence: The death of Lothar II in 871 CE is sometimes presented as a definitive moment in the fragmentation of the Carolingian Empire, but modern scholarship notes that the empire had already been effectively fragmented by the Treaty of Verdun (843 CE). Lothar II's death was significant, but it was one of many succession crises that characterised the period. Additionally, the significance of al-Mu'tamid's assumption of power is sometimes overstated; his attempts to restore central authority were largely unsuccessful, and the Caliphate's fragmentation continued unabated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 871 CE is moderate. Frankish annals provide detailed information for Western and Central Europe. Islamic sources are abundant but often compiled centuries later. Chinese sources are detailed but were compiled much later. The historical record for 871 CE reflects the interests and perspectives of institutional chroniclers. Events in regions without strong written traditions are essentially absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: The death of Lothar II in 871 led to the Treaty of Meerssen (872), which attempted to resolve the succession dispute
The Tang Dynasty's decline, evident in 871, led to its formal collapse in 907 CE
Al-Mu'tamid's failed attempts to restore central authority contributed to the conditions that allowed the Fatimid Caliphate to establish itself in 909
